title: A Unified Critique Framework for Signed Language Writing, Encoding, and Representation
subtitle: A methodological guide for evaluating literature, proposals, and standards
summary: A reusable evaluation framework for signed language writing systems, encodings, standards, and representation proposals.
abstract: This framework provides a reusable method for evaluating signed language writing systems, encodings, standards, and representation proposals without collapsing distinct layers into a single judgment. It separates language, writing system, notation, semantic representation, encoding, rendering, empirical grounding, and institutional infrastructure, then asks what each source actually claims to solve. The framework is especially useful for assessing SignWriting-related literature because SignWriting exposes recurring confusions between symbols and written signs, serialization and writing, encoding and rendering, and technical feasibility and durable text infrastructure. Its purpose is not to dismiss alternative approaches, but to preserve useful insights while identifying level confusion, overreach, and missing infrastructure requirements.
synopsis: This entry provides the shared evaluative framework for the review series. It distinguishes language, writing, notation, semantic representation, encoding, rendering, empirical grounding, and institutional infrastructure, then gives a reusable method for evaluating papers, theses, proposals, and standards without collapsing distinct layers into opinion.

slides.title: Why A Framework
slides.summary: Signed-language writing proposals are often misread when writing, notation, encoding, rendering, evidence, and infrastructure are collapsed.
slides.speech: This framework exists because signed-language writing proposals often mix layers. A source may be useful about notation, weak about writing, strong about encoding, or incomplete about infrastructure. The framework separates those questions before judging the work.

image_prompts.concrete: Methodology slide showing signed-language writing claims separated into writing, notation, encoding, rendering, evidence, and infrastructure layers
image_prompts.abstract: Overlapping claims sorted into clear transparent layers

slides.title: Four Distinctions
slides.summary: The method depends on separating writing from encoding, symbols from written signs, serialization from linear writing, and notation from writing.
bullets: Writing system, encoding, and rendering differ
A symbol is not a written sign
Notation, writing, and semantic representation differ
slides.speech: Four distinctions make the framework necessary. Writing, encoding, and rendering are not the same. A symbol is not the same as a written sign. Serialization is not evidence that writing is linear. Notation, writing, semantic representation, and software representation are different targets.
slides.notes: This is the core teaching slide.

image_prompts.concrete: Four-panel academic slide separating writing from encoding, symbols from written signs, serialization from linear writing, and notation from writing
image_prompts.abstract: Four precise distinctions opening a clean review framework

slides.title: Evaluation Layers
slides.summary: The framework evaluates layer identification, representation model, structural unit analysis, spatial organization, text model, human-machine layers, evidence, and claims.
slides.speech: The framework asks recurring questions. What layer is the source solving? What representation model does it assume? What is the primary written unit? How does it treat space, text, machines, evidence, and institutional requirements?
slides.notes: Keep the list usable, not exhaustive.

image_prompts.concrete: Layered evaluation checklist for signed-language writing systems, with rows for representation, structural unit, spatial organization, text model, evidence, and claims
image_prompts.abstract: A vertical stack of review layers forming a reusable analytic instrument

slides.title: Output Classification
slides.summary: The framework classifies claims as correct and useful, partially correct, misleading, or fundamentally misframed.
slides.speech: The framework also needs a way to classify outputs. Some claims are correct and useful. Some are partially correct. Some are misleading because they move a valid insight to the wrong layer. Some are fundamentally misframed. The classification keeps critique from becoming all-or-nothing.
slides.notes: This slide makes the method more usable. It gives readers a vocabulary for judgment without flattening every reviewed source into right or wrong.

image_prompts.concrete: Four-category review classification slide: correct and useful, partially correct, misleading, and fundamentally misframed, shown as a scholarly decision tree
image_prompts.abstract: A careful evaluation rubric sorting claims by usefulness and fit

slides.title: Final Principle
slides.summary: The goal is constructive critique: preserve what each source sees clearly while refusing to let one layer become the whole answer.
bullets: Use critique to build better infrastructure
slides.speech: The final principle is constructive. The review should preserve what each source sees clearly while refusing to let one layer become the whole answer. That is how critique becomes useful for future writing systems, tools, standards, and research.

image_prompts.concrete: Constructive critique slide showing useful source insights preserved while overextended claims are bounded by layer discipline
image_prompts.abstract: A review frame preserving valid insights while preventing one layer from becoming the whole answer
